content.text: Every TypeScript project is controlled by a

file. It tells the TypeScript compiler (tsc) which files to include, how strict to be, what JavaScript to emit, and how to resolve imports. Understanding tsconfig.json is essential for Angular development because the Angular CLI generates several of them and relies on their settings for type-checking, building, and testing.

attrs.alt: tsconfig.json field map: compilerOptions groups, file selection, extends, and references

content.text: The diagram above organises the main tsconfig fields. The largest section — compilerOptions — is subdivided into output settings, strictness flags, module resolution, and Angular-specific options. The outer fields (include, exclude, files, extends, references) control which files are compiled and how tsconfigs relate to one another.
Generate a tsconfig.json with sensible defaults by running:

This creates a tsconfig.json with every option listed as a comment. Most options are commented out and only the most fundamental ones are active. Angular CLI projects generate a more opinionated tsconfig.json as part of
tsconfig.json has four top-level fields besides compilerOptions:
specifies glob patterns for files to compile. TypeScript supports
are treated as declaration files, not source files.
removes files from the set produced by
accepts an explicit list of file paths to compile. Unlike
, it does not accept globs. Use it only for very small projects or when you need to compile exactly one entry point.
makes one tsconfig inherit from another. The child's settings override the parent's settings for any fields that appear in both.
sets the JavaScript version that tsc emits. TypeScript will downlevel syntax that is not available in the target — for example, if target is ES5, optional chaining (
) is compiled to explicit null checks.
Node.js 16+; adds top-level await, class fields
Latest JS features — appropriate for bundlers that handle downlevelling

Angular 18 projects use ES2022 by default. The Angular CLI builds with esbuild which handles browser compatibility independently of the TypeScript target, so ES2022 is safe even for older browser support.
controls the module format tsc emits. It does not affect which module format you
Browser bundles (Webpack, Vite, esbuild) that handle modules
in tsconfig.json and let the Angular CLI bundler convert them for the browser.
prevents tsc from accidentally including files above
(e.g. a tsconfig.json at the root). If tsc emits to unexpected locations, ensure
file. These maps let the browser debugger show your original TypeScript source at runtime. Angular CLI sets this automatically for development builds.
The single most important option in tsconfig.json is
. It enables a bundle of type-checking rules that catch real bugs:
as if they have properties; forces explicit null handling
Variables with no type annotation defaulting to
Passing a function with wider parameter types where narrower ones are expected
Class properties declared but not assigned in the constructor
inside a function where its type is unknown

Always start new projects with strict: true. Adding it to an existing project is painful because it surfaces hundreds of latent errors at once. Angular CLI sets strict: true by default since Angular 12.
statements to actual files. The default changed between TypeScript versions:
Classic Node.js algorithm: check for file, then index.ts, then package.json main
Modern Node.js: respects package.json exports field; requires .js extensions in relative imports
For bundlers: allows extensionless imports, respects package.json exports without .js extension requirement
because the Angular CLI's esbuild-based build handles resolution.
option maps import alias prefixes to filesystem paths, letting you write short imports instead of deeply relative ones:

Paths are a TypeScript-only feature — the runtime (browser/Node.js) does not understand them. For a browser project, your bundler must be configured to mirror the same aliases. Angular CLI picks up these aliases automatically from tsconfig.json for builds.
for CommonJS packages that lack a default export. Angular projects enable it by default.
A typical Angular project has three tsconfig files that extend each other:

tsconfig.json           ← root; applies to all compilation
├── tsconfig.app.json   ← extends root; app source only (no test files)
└── tsconfig.spec.json  ← extends root; test files only
) from leaking into the application's type namespace. Without this, you could accidentally write
in a component file and TypeScript would not flag it as an error.
Emits type metadata for dependency injection. Angular 14+ uses its own compiler; not needed for most apps.
Without this, class field assignments would override decorator metadata. Angular requires
Makes browser APIs (document, fetch) and ES2022 types (Array.at, structuredClone) available
Skips type-checking .d.ts files in node_modules — significant speed improvement
when using libraries that rely on reflect-metadata (some older NgRx patterns). Check your library documentation.
Several tools help catch tsconfig mistakes:
Print the resolved configuration including inherited settings from extends:

npx tsc --showConfig
# Shows all active options after merging tsconfig.json and any extended files
Run a full type-check without producing output files. Fast and safe for CI. If you see unexpected errors, cross-check the active options with

npx tsc --noEmit
# Exits 0 (success) if no type errors; non-zero otherwise
npm package provides community-maintained tsconfig presets for common environments. You can extend them as a starting point:
Implicit any types go undetected; null errors at runtime
Compiled .js files land next to .ts files, polluting source directories
Files outside rootDir cause TS5090 error
Set rootDir to the common ancestor of all .ts files
Angular CLI may warn about unsupported target; decorators may emit incorrectly
Type-checking all node_modules slows builds significantly
App builds fine in TypeScript but fails at runtime
Add matching aliases to angular.json or webpack config
option controls which built-in JavaScript and browser APIs TypeScript knows about. Without the right lib entries, TypeScript will report errors when you use standard APIs like
Latest TC39 proposals — use with caution as they may not be in runtimes yet
Angular sets lib to ["ES2022", "DOM", "DOM.Iterable"] by default. For Node.js-only projects (no DOM), remove DOM and add @types/node instead:
By default, TypeScript automatically includes all
(empty array) to exclude test framework globals (
) from the application code. If you set
in tsconfig.json (the root), every sub-tsconfig inherits it unless they override it.
For large Angular workspaces or TypeScript monorepos, enabling incremental compilation can reduce full type-check time by 50–80% on subsequent runs.
file stores a serialised version of the type graph. On the next
run, TypeScript reads this file and only re-analyses files whose content or dependencies changed. Commit a
— it is a build artefact, not source.
Angular CLI uses its own incremental build cache (.angular/) and manages tsBuildInfo internally. You do not need to configure incremental manually in Angular projects — it is already on.
Angular 1–16 uses the legacy TypeScript decorator proposal. Angular 17+ supports both legacy and the new TC39 decorator standard. The tsconfig settings differ between them:
Legacy Decorators (Angular ≤ 16 and most Angular 17 projects)
is required because legacy decorators rely on TypeScript's own class field implementation. If you set it to
(the JavaScript standard default), Angular decorators may fail to observe property assignments in component classes.

Angular 17 introduced support for the new TC39 decorator proposal (stage 3 at publication). The Angular CLI warns if you mix the two styles. For new projects started with Angular 17+, check the generated tsconfig.json — Angular CLI sets the correct option for the chosen decorator style.
In an Nx or Angular CLI workspace with multiple applications and libraries, a common pattern is a root tsconfig.base.json that defines shared settings, with per-project tsconfigfile extending it:

tsconfig.base.json       ← shared compiler options + path aliases
apps/
  admin/tsconfig.json    ← extends tsconfig.base.json
  portal/tsconfig.json   ← extends tsconfig.base.json
libs/
  ui/tsconfig.json       ← extends tsconfig.base.json
  data/tsconfig.json     ← extends tsconfig.base.json

All inter-library imports (e.g. import from '@myorg/ui') are resolved through the paths aliases in tsconfig.base.json. When adding a new library, add its alias once to tsconfig.base.json and it is immediately available in all extending configs.
When TypeScript cannot find a module or resolves it from an unexpected location, the
flag prints a step-by-step log of the resolution algorithm:

npx tsc --noEmit --traceResolution 2>&1 | grep -A5 "Resolving module '@myorg/ui'"

# Output:
# Resolving module '@myorg/ui' from 'src/app/app.component.ts'.
# Explicitly specified module resolution kind: 'Bundler'.
# 'paths' option is specified, looking for a pattern to match module name '@myorg/ui'.
# Module name '@myorg/ui' matched pattern '@myorg/ui'.
# Trying substitution 'libs/ui/src/index.ts', candidate module location: ...
# File 'libs/ui/src/index.ts' exists - use it.
This trace is essential when debugging 'Cannot find module' errors that appear only during CI or after changing tsconfig paths.
tsconfig.json is the single source of truth for how the TypeScript compiler processes your project. Always enable
to match your runtime environment — Angular uses ES2022 and ESNext respectively. Use
for clean import aliases, and use
to share common settings across tsconfig.app.json and tsconfig.spec.json. Run
in CI to catch type errors without producing files, and use
